metadata.title: Student Loan Math: Apply the Matching Principle Before You Borrow

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The matching principle, checking annual debt service against a ten-percent ceiling, reframed borrowing as a decision rather than a number for me. I want to run my own figures through the amortization formula. Can you (1) compute the monthly payment, total paid, and total interest for a 40k loan at 5.5% over ten years, (2) test it against a 45k starting salary using the ten-percent ceiling, and (3) find the exact starting salary at which that loan's debt service lands right on the ceiling?

props.command: The matching principle, checking annual debt service against a ten-percent ceiling, reframed borrowing as a decision rather than a number for me. I want to run my own figures through the amortization formula. Can you (1) compute the monthly payment, total paid, and total interest for a 40k loan at 5.5% over ten years, (2) test it against a 45k starting salary using the ten-percent ceiling, and (3) find the exact starting salary at which that loan's debt service lands right on the ceiling?
props.segment: Student Loan Math: Apply the Matching

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Most students borrow a number, not a decision. The number comes from the financial aid office; the decision requires knowing what that number costs over ten years and whether the starting salary you expect can service it. The matching principle is the rule of thumb used in accounting and in personal finance: match the cost to the benefit over the same time horizon. Applied to student loans, it asks: does the income this degree produces match the debt it requires to produce it?
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'You borrowed a number. Did you make a decision?' — matching principle stated as income vs debt over same horizon; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: One command. Give Claude two loan scenarios — thirty thousand at five percent and sixty thousand at six percent, both on ten-year repayment — and ask it to calculate monthly payment, total paid, and total interest for each, then apply the matching principle for a forty-five thousand dollar starting salary.

props.command: claude "Calculate and compare two student loan scenarios: Scenario A: $30,000 at 5% APR, 10-year repayment. Scenario B: $60,000 at 6% APR, 10-year repayment. For each: monthly payment, total paid, total interest paid. Apply matching principle for $45,000 starting salary. Add 'Forgone optionality' column. Format as markdown table."

beats.narration_text: Read the formula before trusting the number. The amortization formula is P times r times one-plus-r to the n, divided by one-plus-r-to-the-n minus one. P is principal, r is monthly rate, n is number of payments. This is the formula every lender uses. The matching principle adds one more check: annual debt service as a fraction of gross income — the conventional ceiling is ten percent.

props.code:
# loan_comparison.py — Claude Code output

monthly_payment = P * r * (1+r)**n / ((1+r)**n - 1)  # amortization formula

# Scenario A: P=30000, r=0.05/12, n=120
# monthly = $318.20 | total = $38,184 | interest = $8,184

# Scenario B: P=60000, r=0.06/12, n=120  
# monthly = $666.00 | total = $79,920 | interest = $19,920

# Matching principle @ $45,000 salary:
# Annual debt service A: $3,818 = 8.5% of gross — within 10% ceiling
# Annual debt service B: $7,992 = 17.8% of gross — EXCEEDS ceiling
# Forgone optionality B: ~$4,174/yr less discretionary income for 10 yrs

beats.narration_text: The table lands. Scenario A stays under the ten-percent matching ceiling — eight-point-five percent of gross income. Scenario B at sixty thousand breaks through it: seventeen-point-eight percent of gross income, every month for ten years. The forgone optionality column names what that gap costs in reduced financial flexibility per year.
beats.visual_intent: screen recording of the two-scenario markdown table — Scenario B row flagged with EXCEEDS, forgone optionality column visible

beats.narration_text: Now find the break-even. Ask Claude: at what loan amount does the matching principle break for a forty-five thousand dollar starting salary — what is the maximum debt load that stays at or below ten percent of gross annual income?

props.command: claude "Calculate: what is the maximum student loan amount (at 5.5% APR, 10-year term) that keeps annual debt service at or below 10% of a $45,000 starting salary? Show the math and express the answer as both a dollar amount and as a fraction of one year's gross income."

beats.narration_text: The break-even answer: at five-point-five percent APR over ten years, the maximum loan that stays under the matching ceiling for a forty-five thousand dollar salary is approximately forty-two thousand dollars — roughly one year of expected gross income. That is the conventional rule of thumb the matching principle produces when you run the numbers.
beats.visual_intent: screen recording: break-even calculation output — '$42,000 max = 10% ceiling at $45K salary' — expressed as 1x annual income rule of thumb

beats.narration_text: The matching principle converts a borrowing decision into a math problem with a checkable answer. The ten-percent ceiling is a conventional threshold, not a law — but it is derived from the same logic lenders use. Scenario A passes. Scenario B fails. The break-even is one year of expected gross income. Those three numbers are the decision.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'Max debt ≈ 1× expected starting salary' — Scenario A passes, Scenario B fails; the 10% ceiling stated explicitly

beats.narration_text: Your move: pull your actual loan balance and interest rate from your student aid account. Run this prompt with your real numbers. If you are already over the matching ceiling for your expected starting salary, that is not a crisis — it is information. And the first thing to do with information is stop making the gap larger.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) pull your actual loan balance, 2) run matching principle calculation, 3) if over ceiling — stop borrowing more

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The matching principle converts a borrowing decision into a math problem with a checkable answer. The ten-percent ceiling is a conventional threshold, not a law — but it is derived from the same logic lenders use. Scenario A passes. Scenario B fails. The break-even is one year of expected gross income. Those three numbers are the decision. Your move: pull your actual loan balance and interest rate from your student aid account. Run this prompt with your real numbers. If you are already over the matching ceiling for your expected starting salary, that is not a crisis — it is information. And the first thing to do with information is stop making the gap larger.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Student Loan Math: Apply the Matching Principle Before You Borrow] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
